coredata.dc:title: Probing pyrolysis conversion of separator from spent lithium-ion batteries: Thermal behavior, kinetics, evolved gas analysis and aspen plus modeling
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Zhitong
Romano, Pietro
Fan, Weilin
Gautam, Shina
Tippayawong, Nakorn
Jaroenkhasemmeesuk, Chawannat
Liu, Jie
Wang, Xiaobo
Qi, Wei
coredata.dc:description: The exponential growth in the consumption of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) has resulted in a concomitant increase in the spent LIBs. Multi-component recycling is a highly desirable and beneficial process, yet the separator component remains overlooked. This study examined the thermal behavior, kinetics, thermodynamics, and evolved products of separator pyrolysis in N2 and CO2 atmospheres. Furthermore, the conversion was modeled using Aspen Plus and a sensitivity analysis was conducted to investigate the impact of varying operating conditions. The findings indicated that the whole degradation process could be divided into three stages: below 200 °C, 200–550 °C and 550–900 °C. The evolved products at peak temperatures of 485 °C in N2 and 500 °C in CO2 were composed of olefins and alkanes. The average apparent activation energy values were determined to be 222.78 kJ mol−1 and 159.36 kJ mol−1 in the N2 and CO2 atmospheres, respectively. Thermodynamic analysis indicated that the separator decomposition was an endothermic and non-spontaneous process. Aspen Plus modeling results were in agreement with the experimental data. The sensitivity analysis revealed that an increase in temperature lead to an increased content of lighter compounds (≤C9) and a decrease in the proportion of heavier compounds (>C10) in the pyrolysis products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Separator
Thermo-chemical conversion
Aspen plus modeling
E-waste

Comparison of theoretical and experimental curves.

Comparison between experimental and modeling results.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proximately and ultimate analysis of separator.

head.ce:title: Probing pyrolysis conversion of separator from spent lithium-ion batteries: Thermal behavior, kinetics, evolved gas analysis and aspen plus modeling

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The exponential growth in the consumption of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) has resulted in a concomitant increase in the spent LIBs. Multi-component recycling is a highly desirable and beneficial process, yet the separator component remains overlooked. This study examined the thermal behavior, kinetics, thermodynamics, and evolved products of separator pyrolysis in N2 and CO2 atmospheres. Furthermore, the conversion was modeled using Aspen Plus and a sensitivity analysis was conducted to investigate the impact of varying operating conditions. The findings indicated that the whole degradation process could be divided into three stages: below 200 °C, 200–550 °C and 550–900 °C. The evolved products at peak temperatures of 485 °C in N2 and 500 °C in CO2 were composed of olefins and alkanes. The average apparent activation energy values were determined to be 222.78 kJ mol−1 and 159.36 kJ mol−1 in the N2 and CO2 atmospheres, respectively. Thermodynamic analysis indicated that the separator decomposition was an endothermic and non-spontaneous process. Aspen Plus modeling results were in agreement with the experimental data. The sensitivity analysis revealed that an increase in temperature lead to an increased content of lighter compounds (≤C9) and a decrease in the proportion of heavier compounds (>C10) in the pyrolysis products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing global awareness of climate change, primarily attributed to the combustion of fossil fuels, is accelerating the development of low-carbon energy technologies [1]. It is imperative that the global transition to renewable energy sources is achieved in order to prevent a global temperature increase in excess of 2 °C compared to pre-industrial levels. The inherent intermittency of renewable energy sources has prompted the integration of energy storage devices. Among the available energy storage technologies, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have attracted considerable attention due to their high energy density, environmental compatibility, and reusability. These batteries are utilized in a diverse array of devices, spanning from compact portable electronics to high-capacity electric vehicles and stationary energy storage systems. It is projected that global sales of electric vehicles will reach 150–900 million units, with production of electric vehicles expected to reach 3 billion units by 2050 [2]. However, the rapid increase in the consumption of LIBs has resulted in a growing number of spent LIBs. Given the negative environmental impact of these materials and the potential to recover valuable metals, recycling this waste is highly desirable and beneficial [3,4].

The prevailing approach to recycling technologies has been to prioritise the recovery of valuable metals from spent LIBs [5], with relatively little attention paid to the organic components. Of these, the separator constitutes approximately 3–5% of the total weight of LIBs. The majority of commercially available separators are polyolefin membranes composed of semi-crystalline polyethylene (PE) or polypropylene (PP) [6]. Natarajan et al. [7] demonstrated a simple recovery process for used separators, comprising rinsing and subsequent reuse in battery manufacturing. Furthermore, a nanocomposite thin film was fabricated using a recovered separator and graphite [8]. Bhar et al. [9] produced turbostratic carbon from separators for utilization as an anode material in LIBs. Natarajan et al. [10] synthesized hollow carbon spheres and reduced graphene oxide from separators for the storage of H2 and CO2. Despite some researches efforts in the field of separator recycling, the component remains a largely neglected element entering the waste stream in significant quantities.

The thermo-chemical conversion process offers significant advantages in terms of waste reduction and potential energy recovery [11,12]. However, there is a paucity of research in this area. Furthermore, the properties and yields of the products are influenced by the atmospheres. Among the various carrier gases, the utilization of CO2 has the potential to confer benefits to both the economy and environment [13]. Nevertheless, there has been a lack of comprehensive research in this area, which has impeded the implementation of practical large-scale operations. Thermogravimetric analysis is a simple and expeditious thermoanalytical technique for quantifying the mass loss of solids in response to temperature changes, rendering it a valuable tool for investigating pyrolysis behavior. The integration of a thermogravimetric gas chromatography-mass spectrometry coupled with a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (TG-FTIR-GC/MS) system enables the simultaneous analysis of both macroscopic analysis and detailed compound distribution through evolved gas analysis. Moreover, a simulation tool is required to assess the performance of industrial-scale plants, as experimental data is only available from pilot-scale plants. Aspen Plus is a software program that employs powerful algorithms and a comprehensive database to facilitate the construction of models for calculating mass and energy balances of units, as well as for the analysis and optimization of process performance. While there are several reports on the modelling of pyrolysis for coal, biomass and tyres using Aspen Plus [14–16], no reports were not found on the modeling of separators. Therefore, this study aims to examine the thermal behavior, kinetics, thermodynamics and evolved gas of separator pyrolysis in two distinct atmospheres. Validation of the pyrolysis conversion is also achieved through the use of Aspen Plus modeling. It is anticipated that the findings of this study will provide invaluable in advancing the field of spent LIBs recycling, while also contributing to the sustainable development of LIBs industry.

The spent LIBs were obtained from a local cellular phone repair shop. Prior to utilization, the LIBs underwent discharge using a 10 wt% NaCl solution, thus minimizing the potential risk of short-circuiting or explosions of LIBs. Subsequently, the drying process was followed by manual dismantling, where the separator was specifically separated. The separators were then reduced to a fine powder by means of a rotary pulverizer (FM200, Beijing Geruideman Instrument Equipment Co. Ltd., China). Prior to undertaking the subsequent tests, the samples were subjected to sieving process using a no. 100 sieve.

Thermogravimetric experiments were conducting utilising a thermogravimetric analyser (TGA, TG 209F3, Netzsch). Approximately 10 mg of separator samples were loaded into the crucibles and subjected to controlled heating under N2 and CO2 atmospheres with a flow rate of 30 ml min−1, respectively. The experimental procedures were repeated at four different heating rates of 10, 20, 30 and 40 K min−1, respectively. To ensure the reliability of the results, each individual test was performed at least three times. To analyze the evolved products during thermal decomposition, on-line testing was performed using the thermogravimetric analyzer coupled with a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (TG-FTIR) and a thermogravimetric gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (TG-GC/MS) system.

The fundamental principles of kinetic models for solids are outlined here, with additional details available in the referenced literatures [17–20]. Pyrolysis conversion represents a complex mechanism, comprising a multitude of sequential and simultaneous reactions. Model-free approaches, including Friedman, Kissinger-Akahira-Sunose (KAS), and Flynn-Wall-Ozawa (FWO), are regarded as reliable methods for studying kinetics and assessing the associated complexity. Consequently, three linear methods were employed: Friedman, KAS, and FWO models. The formulas for these parameters are presented in Table 1 .

The Master-plots method has been shown to be a reliable tool for determining the mechanisms of solid-state reactions, with its expression described as eq. (1). It is based on the comparison between theoretical and experimental curves. The theoretical master plot is obtained by plotting

In addition to the kinetic triplets, thermodynamic parameters are of significant importance in the evaluation of feasibility and energy requirements of thermo-chemical conversions. The change in enthalpy (ΔH) is a useful indicator of whether a particular reaction is endothermic or exothermic. The change in entropy (ΔS) reflects the degree of disorder in a process and can be either positive or negative. The change in Gibbs free energy (ΔG) of the system that occurs during a reaction is therefore equal to ΔH minus ΔS. It represents the change in available energy and indicates the spontaneity of a system during the reaction. The aforementioned thermodynamic parameters are determined by the following equations [21,22].
(2)
Δ H =
E a
− R ∙
T p
(3)
Δ G =
E a
+ R ·
T p
ln
k b
T p
h A
(4)
Δ S =
Δ H − Δ G
T p
where kb is the Boltzmann constant (1.38E-23 J K−1); h and Tp represent the Planck constant (6.63E-34 Js) and the peak temperature (K) in derivative thermogravimetry, respectively.

The proximate and ultimate analysis for separator is presented in Table 2 . The proximate analysis indicated a relatively high volatile matter content of 72.82 wt% and a comparatively low ash content of 23.86 wt%. The Vad value was found to be comparable with that of biomass 60–80 % [23], indicating it as an ideal material for fuel generation. The elevated ash content can be attributed to the Al2O3 coating on separator, which enhances its thermal stability, wettability and capacity for liquid electrolyte uptake [24] The ultimate analysis revealed that the contents of C, H, O, N and S and were 61.94, 9.37, 6.25, 0.26 and 0.11 wt%, respectively. The reduced levels of N and S may contribute to a reduction in the environmental risk of releasing these compounds into the atmosphere. The lower O content indicated that pyrolysis oil was a more valuable source of energy than biomass.

The FTIR spectrum of the separator sample is illustrated in Fig. S1. The absorbance observed at 3441 cm−1 was attributed to the O-H stretching vibration. Two distinct bands were observed at 2916 and 2847 cm−1, which were assigned to the C-H stretching vibrations. The absorbance at 1777 and 1638 cm−1 were due to the C=O stretching in carbonate functional groups with ethylene carbonate and the C-C stretching vibration, respectively. The bands at 1473-1408 and 607 cm−1 were identified as the -CH2 scissoring vibration and ring deformation vibration, respectively. The absorbances at 1192 and 1083 cm−1 were corresponded to C-O stretching in organic solvents, such as ethylene carbonate [25]. The bands at 849, 711 and 641 cm−1 were ascribed to the C-C ring stretching, C-O-C stretching and COO- wagging modes of solvents, respectively [26].

In the field of thermo-analytical chemistry, differential calorimetry scanning (DSC) is a method that employs a refence sample to determine the difference in heat required to increase the temperature of a sample. When the polymer was subjected to a thermal cycle, it exhibited an endothermic reaction during the heating phase and an exothermic reaction during the cooling phase. The DSC thermograph of separator sample is shown in Fig. S2. During the heating cycle, an endothermic peak was observed at 139. 2 °C with a melting enthalpy of 117.0 J g−1 was observed. In the cooling cycle, an exothermic peak was detected at 117.7 °C with an enthalpy of 115.8 J g−1. This finding was consistent with the melting temperature of ∼135 °C and the crystallization temperature of ∼115 °C for high density polyethylene (HDPE) [27,28]. It was noteworthy that there were two minor peaks at 103.1 °C and 104.2 °C with enthalpy of 122.0 J g−1 during the heating cycling, which was not similar with that for pure PE matrix. The aforementioned slight peaks were primarily attributed to the loss of bound water in LiNO3·3H2O [29,30].

Fig. 1 illustrates the TG-DTG profiles of separator at four distinct heating rates of 10, 20, 30, and 40 K min−1. From the TG curves presented in Fig. 1a and c, it can be observed the total mass loss was in the ranges of 60.3–63.0 wt% and 62.5–65.9 wt% in N2 and CO2 atmospheres, respectively. A comparison of the TG curves at different heating rates revealed a shift towards higher temperatures with increasing heating rates. This was primarily due to the constraints imposed by heat and mass transfer, which resulted in the formation of temperature gradients within the sample particles. The entire degradation process could be divided into three stages on the basis of the mass loss and peak temperatures. In the initial stage (<200 °C), water evaporation dominated with a small mass loss of 3.40–5.14 wt% in N2 and 1.99–3.69 wt% in CO2 atmosphere. This value exceeded the Mad of 1.05 wt%, indicating that minimal volatile matter was also released during this stage. In stage II (200–550 °C), a greater weight loss was observed, with a range of 54.38–55.90 wt% N2 and 58.5–59.7 wt% in CO2 atmosphere. It was attributed to the decomposition of PE and the evaporation of volatile compounds. The peaks in the DTG curves were found to be 470–500 °C and 485–525 °C in the N2 and CO2 atmospheres, respectively. The degradation of plastics occurred predominantly as a single step, as indicated by the presence of a single peak. Alvarenga et al. [31] demonstrated that the degradation of PE occurred within the temperature range of 400–550 °C. Sadd et al. [32] reported peak temperatures of 480 °C and 474 °C under N2 and CO2 atmospheres for low density polyethylene (LDPE). Li et al. [33] identified a temperature range of 464.1–540.2 °C for PE decomposition. Furthermore, the weight loss exceeded 90 wt% with minimal residue remaining. The value was considerably higher than that observed in the present study, which was attributed to the presence of coated Al2O3 and residual electrolyte in the separator. With regard to stage III (550–900 °C), a slight weight loss of 0.50–3.29 and 2.01–3.42 wt% was observed in the N2 and CO2 atmospheres, respectively. It was mainly attributed to the formation of char and the decomposition of electrolyte. The weight loss in CO2 atmosphere was comparatively larger than that in N2 atmosphere, which resulted from the char gasification.

Fig. 2 depicts the 3D and 2D-FTIR spectra of the evolved products resulting from separator decomposition. From Fig. 2a and b, a prominent peak was identified in the wavenumber regions of 2980-2788 cm−1, which corresponded to the C-H stretching vibration. The absorption bands observed at 4000-3500 and 1875-1600 cm−1 were attributed to the OH and C=O stretching vibrations, respectively [34]. Morover, vibrations at 1570-1412 and 1093-792 cm−1 were assigned to C=C stretching and C-O/C-C stretching vibrations, respectively [35]. Observing the spectrum along with time in Fig. 2a, significant peaks were observed between 1250 and 1550 s (415–515 °C), which correlated with the TG-DTG analysis. In contrast, significant peaks were identified at 2387-2225 and 716-612 cm−1 in the CO2 atmosphere. These were attributed to the asymmetric stretching and double degenerate bending modes of CO2, respectively [36]. Additional, the presence of OH, C=O, C=C and C-O/C-C bands confirmed. It was observed that the intensity of CO2 decreased significantly at the end of the stage II in Fig. 1, which was attributed to the onset of gasification reaction.

A further investigation into the composition of evolved products was conduced using GC-MS analysis, with the resulting total ion chromatograms presented in Fig. 3 and Table 3 . It can be seen that the principal components were analogous in N2 and CO2, composing of olefins and alkanes. This finding was consistent with that of previous reports. Zheng et al. [37] showed that the products of thermal cracking reaction of LDPE comprised about 57.87 % of C7-C9, 27.31 % of C10-C16 and 14.81 % of C17-C22, respectively. Fu et al. [38] conducted an investigation in to the distribution of products resulting from the PE pyrolysis at 500 °C. The carbon number distribution allowed for the products to be divided into three categories: C1-C5, C6-C23 (paraffins, olefins, and diolefins), and C24+. The respective percentages were 3.3 %, 29.04 % (12.56 %, 15.90 %, and 3.88 %), and 67.66 %, respectively. Furthermore, Zhang et al. [39] showed that the primary products were the long-chain hydrocarbons. However, some differences were observed in the products formed in the 2 atm. For example, cyclopentadecane was identified and heneicosane and tetracosane were present in high content of in CO2. It indicated its lower reactivity at this temperature region, which can be confirmed by the following “3.6 Thermodynamic analysis” section.

A linear fitting was performed within the α range of 0.20–0.90, and the results are present in Fig. S3. Subsequently, the Ea values were calculated from the slopes of the fitted lines in accordance with Table 1 and displayed in Fig. 4 . It can be observed that the values obtained from the three model-free models were comparable, although they exhibited a descending order of FWO > Friedman > KAS. The R2 value is a critical indicator for assessing the reliability of models, with values reaching as high as 0.99. A comparison of the R2 values revealed that they were relatively larger for the FWO model, indicating that this method was more reliable for modeling separator pyrolysis. As an illustrative example, the Ea values derived from the FWO method exhibited an upward trend, which was consistent with the findings of previous reports [40,41]. The average values were found to be 222.78 and 159.36 kJ mol−1 in N2 and CO2 atmosphere, respectively. Aboulkas et al. [42] reported Ea values of 238–247 kJ mol−1 and 215–221 kJ mol−1 for the pyrolysis of HDPE and LDPE in N2. Zheng et al. [37] determined a value of 249.87 kJ mol−1 for LDPE pyrolysis in an Ar atmosphere. Shan et al. [43] obtained Ea values of 260–305 and 282–352 kJ mol−1 in N2 by employing the KAS and Friedman methods, respectively. In the CO2 atmosphere, the values decreased to 214–227 and 258–280 kJ mol−1 in, respectively. This trend was consistent with the findings of the present study, which indicated that the lower energy barrier was overcome more readily in a CO2 atmosphere [44]. The reduction in activation energy was primarily due to the reaction of char with CO2 to facilitate separator decomposition.

Fig. S4 illustrates the thermodynamic values of ΔH, ΔS, and ΔG, which have been calculated using Ea values derived from the FWO model. The value of ΔH represents the energy required to dissociate the complex bonds of PE. A higher values indicate a less reactive system [48]. These values demonstrated an upward trend that aligned with the trend observed for Ea in Fig. 4. It implied that the endothermic nature of the pyrolysis process increased in line with the progression of pyrolysis conversion progressed. The values of ΔH values in an N2 atmosphere demonstrated an increase from 185.5 to 243.82 kJ mol−1 with a mean value of 216.35 kJ mol−1. In comparison, the values increased from 144.50 to 162.25 kJ mol−1 in CO2 atmosphere with a lower mean value of 152.71 kJ mol−1. Furthermore, the aforementioned average ΔH values were found to be comparable to the average Ea values of 222.78 kJ mol−1 in N2 and 159.36 kJ mol−1 in CO2. It can be observed that the discrepancy between ΔH and Ea was around 5 kJ mol−1. This result indicated that the potential energy barriers required for the reaction were low, which was conducive to the formation of activated complex [49,50]. Furthermore, the positive ΔH values indicated that the decomposition of separator necessitated the input of heat in order to elevate the energy level of the reagents to the requisite transition state. The larger ΔH values in N2 indicated that a greater amount of energy was necessary to satisfy the requisite condition for the reaction to proceed. Entropy is a state function of the reaction system, representing the degree of disorder or randomness of energy and matter within the system. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the ΔS values were negative in the CO2 atmosphere, indicating that the degree of disorder of the products formed by bond dissociation was less than that of the reactants. For comparison, the value was positive across the α range in the N2 atmosphere, indicating that the pyrolysis reactivity increased as the reaction progressed [51]. The ΔG provides a comprehensive evaluation of the total energy increase within the system, encompassing both the heat and disorder contributions. The values observed in the N2 atmosphere exhibited a declining trend from 118.79 to 108.07 kJ mol−1 with a mean of 112.75 kJ mol−1. In contrast, they were stable with a mean of 198.78 kJ mol−1. These positive ΔG values indicated a non-spontaneous process for separator decomposition and required external energy for thermal decomposition.

Aspen Plus is one of the most widely utilized commercial process simulators in chemical engineering, facilitating the generation of both prebuilt and user-defined models through the utilization of Fortran and subroutine programming. It was previously modeling that, the pyrolysis process exhibited notable discrepancies in the composition and distributio of the resulting gases, contingent upon the specific operating parameters, including the feedstock properties, residence time, and temperature [52,53]. The simulations demonstrated that changing these parameters could markedly influence the quantity and quality of the gases produced as a consequence of pyrolysis. Sensitivity studies have demonstrated the importance of catalyst and reactor design in optimizing pyrolysis processes for higher product yields and desired material qualities. In the case of pyrolysis, Aspen Plus does not have a pre-designed model and it was necessary to use the integration of some existing blocks. In particular, the pyrolysis process was developed using RYield and RGibbs reactors. Fig. 6 displays the simulation flow sheet for the separator pyrolysis process.

In the extant modeling approach, two component types are employed: unconventional and conventional components. Separators are regarded as non-conventional solids and are modeled using proximate and ultimate analyses. The specific property methods selected for enthalpy and density are the HCOALGEN and DCOALIGT methods, respectively. All constituents determined through experimentation were incorporated into the simulation as conventional constituents. The thermodynamic method employed in this simulation is the Peng-Robinson equation of state with Boston-Matias modifications (PR-BM) [54]. In the simulation, the separators were directed to the RYield reactor (DECOMP). In this process, the aforementioned materials, where they were converted from unconventional components into conventional ones. In the RYIELD reactor, it is necessary to convert the non-conventional feed into the various conventional components that is composed of. To this end, the CALCULATOR block in ASPEN PLUS was employed. The block enables the implementation of a calculation code, written in the Fortran programming language, which is used to determine the composition of the current leaving the RYIELD reactor [54]. This code is important in the conversion process because it performs the necessary calculations to determine the composition of the conventional components. The products of this reactor were sent to the RGibbs reactor (PYRO), where the minimization of the Gibbs free energy and the estimation of the equilibrium composition took place. The output values of this block were related to the thermodynamic equilibrium and can be used to inform expectations regarding the limits of the experimental results.

In order to validate the model employed in the present study to represent the pyrolysis process, a number of results from relevant research studies were examined in order to facilitate comparison and evaluation [37]. Fig. 7 represents a comparison between the results obtained from the modeling and those derived from the experimental investigation. It is evident that the model aligned with the experimental data, although there were some discrepancies. The Aspen model is based on the assumption that the system reaches thermodynamic equilibrium, whereby all chemical reactions are in equilibrium and there are no further changes in product concentrations. Nevertheless, pyrolysis may not reach thermodynamic equilibrium due to short residence times and some kinetically limited reactions. The primary discrepancy between the model and experimental data concerned C > 12 compounds. The developed model permitted a sensitivity analysis to be conducted, with the objective of evaluating the effect of temperature on the percentage of the various compounds at thermodynamic equilibrium. Fig. 8 shows that for compounds of a lower molecular weight, an increase in temperature resulted in an elevated molar fraction within the pyrolysis products. Conversely, for heavier compounds, an increase in temperature resulted in a decrease in the proportion. The aforementioned behavior was observed to be reversed for compounds with C9, whereby an increase in the molar fraction was found when transitioning from a temperature of 300 °C–350 °C, and a decrease for temperatures above 350 °C. A closer examination of the process revealed that thermodynamic equilibrium played a crucial role in the pyrolysis at higher temperatures, which exhibited a tendency to favor products with lower free energy. In particular, the formation of light compounds was found to be thermodynamically more favorable than that of heavy compounds at high temperatures, due to the lower free energy of formation of the former. These results indicated that a temperature of ∼500 °C could be selected for industrial implementation.

A comparative investigation was conducted to examine the pyrolysis conversion of the separator under N2 and CO2 atmospheres. The total mass loss was determined to be 60.3–63.0 wt% and 62.5–65.9 wt% in 2 atm, respectively. The whole degradation process could be divided into three stages including water evaporation, PE decomposition & volatile evaporation, and char formation & electrolyte decomposition. The evolved products observed at theh peaks of 485 °C in N2 and 500 °C in CO2 were identified as olefins and alkanes. However, some differences were observed in the products formed in the 2 atm. For example, Cyclopentadecane was identified in the CO2 atmosphere, while heneicosane and tetracosane were presented in high concentrations. Ea values derived from the three model-free models were found to be comparable, with the average Ea values were determined to be 222.78 kJ mol−1 and 159.36 kJ mol−1 in the N2 and CO2 atmospheres, respectively. The D4 and A2 models were deemed suitable for modeling separator decomposition in 2 atm with the expressions g(α) = 1-(2/3)α-(1-α)2/3 and g(α) = [-ln(1-α)]1/2, respectively. Thermodynamic analysis indicated that the decomposition of the separator occurred as an endothermic and non-spontaneous process. The changes in ΔH values followed the trend of Ea changes and the variation between them was around 5 kJ mol−1. The ΔS values exhibited a positive and negative trend across the α range of 0.2–0.9 in both the N2 and CO2 atmospheres, respectively. The modeling results were found to be in agreement with the experimental data, with the exception of some discrepancies observed for compounds with carbon numbers larger than 12. The sensitivity analysis demonstrated that an increase in temperature from 300 to 600 °C resulted in an elevated concentration of lighter compounds (≤C9) and a reduction in the abundance of heavier compounds (>C10) in the pyrolysis products.

Zhitong Yao: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Pietro Romano: Validation, Data curation. Weilin Fan: Formal analysis. Shina Gautam: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Nakorn Tippayawong: Validation. Chawannat Jaroenkhasemmeesuk: Validation. Jie Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xiaobo Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Wei Qi: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Zhitong Yao reports financial support was provided by Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. LTY21B070002).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Kartal, S. Sezer, U. Ozveren, Investigation of steam and CO2gasification for biochar using a circulating fluidized bed gasifier model in Aspen HYSYS, Journal of CO2 Utilization 62 (2022). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcou.2022.102078.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pyrolysis study of different fruit wastes using an aspen Plus model
Front. Sustain. Food Syst.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. AlNouss, P. Parthasarathy, H.R. Mackey, T. Al-Ansari, G. McKay, Pyrolysis Study of Different Fruit Wastes Using an Aspen Plus Model, Front Sustain Food Syst 5 (2021). https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.604001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ASPEN PLUS predictive simulation of printed circuit boards pyrolysis and steam gasification for organic fraction valorization
Waste

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Romano, E. Melchiorre, F. Veglio, ASPEN PLUS Predictive Simulation of Printed Circuit Boards Pyrolysis and Steam Gasification for Organic Fraction Valorization, Waste 1 (2023). https://doi.org/10.3390/waste1010018.
